summary: User policy for hiding personalized website recommendations from Start. The local Microsoft StartMenu.admx file documents the same Explorer policy key and HideRecommendedPersonalizedSites value name used by the app.

setting.name: Start Personalized Site Recommendations (Current User)
setting.casual_explanation: Windows 11 Start can surface personalized website suggestions. This user policy hides those recommendations just for the current account.
setting.why_people_change_it: People hide it when they want a less personalized Start menu for themselves without forcing the whole device to behave the same way.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Hiding personalized sites makes Start feel less suggestion-driven, but the current user loses those personalized web recommendations.
setting.professional_notes: This is the user-scoped variant of the same Start policy documented in the Start Policy CSP and StartMenu.admx.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: The current user is not forced to hide personalized site recommendations.

allowed_values.label: Personalized sites shown
allowed_values.meaning: Personalized site recommendations remain allowed for the current user.

allowed_values.label: Personalized sites hidden
allowed_values.meaning: Personalized site recommendations are hidden for the current user.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes HideRecommendedPersonalizedSites = 1 under HKCU, which matches the documented hide behavior.

windows_defaults.label: Windows user-policy default
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows 11 version 22H2 and later user profiles

states.rationale: The user policy is not configured by default.

windows_defaults.notes: Not configured corresponds to ordinary Windows behavior for the current user.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: Users who want the ordinary Start experience
avoid_for: Users who want fewer personalized Start suggestions
recommended_profiles.good_when: You want Windows to keep handling personalized site suggestions normally for your account.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want a less personalized Start menu for yourself.
tradeoffs: Personalized site suggestions can remain visible.

recommended_profiles.label: Less personalized Start for current user
intended_for: Users who want fewer personalized suggestions in Start
avoid_for: Users who like Start surfacing personalized websites

states.rationale: Hide personalized site recommendations for the current user.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want your own Start menu to feel less personalized.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You use those recommendations as shortcuts.
tradeoffs: Personalized site suggestions disappear for your account.
Other accounts can keep their own behavior if no device policy overrides it.

evidence.title: Microsoft Start Policy CSP
evidence.summary: Official documentation for HideRecommendedSection and HideRecommendedPersonalizedSites, including allowed values and scope.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft StartMenu.admx mapping
evidence.summary: The local ADMX file maps HideRecommendedSection and HideRecommendedPersonalizedSites to the Explorer policy key with both user and machine scope.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes HideRecommendedPersonalizedSites = 1 under HKCU for this tweak.

evidence.title: Existing tweak provenance record
evidence.summary: The repo already tracks provenance for this tweak in the current catalog output.

decision.why: The local Microsoft StartMenu.admx file explicitly defines HideRecommendedPersonalizedSites under Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Explorer with class="Both", so the same value name is valid for both HKLM and HKCU. The official Start Policy CSP documents the 0/1 semantics for the same policy.

validation_proof.notes: The local official Microsoft ADMX file names the same Explorer policy key and HideRecommendedPersonalizedSites value used by the app. Because the policy is class="Both", the same surface is valid for HKCU as well as HKLM. The official Start Policy CSP provides the 0/1 shown-versus-hidden semantics.
